title: Trump to have annual physical on May 26, White House says
description: Trump to have annual physical on May 26, White House says
fullText:
(Corrects to add year 2025, to clarify start of Trump’s term, in paragraph 2)

May 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump will have his annual physical on May 26 at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center, the White House announced on Monday.

At 79, Trump’s health remains a subject of public scrutiny. He has maintained a busy schedule since assuming office in January 2025 and frequently spends weekends golfing at his clubs in Florida and Virginia.

Trump said he underwent a CT scan during an October visit to Walter Reed to rule out cardiovascular issues and said the test showed no abnormalities. He later said he regretted disclosing the scan because it prompted speculation about his health.

More recently, photographs showing bruising or discoloration on his hands have raised additional questions.

The White House said Trump will also use the visit to meet with service members and staff at the military hospital.

title: Bathurst Resources Q3 FY26 slides: cash strong despite revenue dip
description: Bathurst Resources Q3 FY26 slides: cash strong despite revenue dip
fullText:
Bathurst Resources Limited (ASX:BRL) presented its third-quarter fiscal 2026 results on May 12, 2026, revealing a challenging operational environment offset by a robust balance sheet and advancing growth initiatives. The New Zealand-based metallurgical coal producer reported consolidated revenue of NZD $182 million for the nine months ended March 31, 2026, down 8% from NZD $197 million in the prior-year period, while maintaining full-year EBITDA guidance between NZD $35-45 million.

The company’s stock responded positively to the update, rising 7.27% to close at AUD $0.59 on the presentation date, suggesting investor confidence in management’s strategic pivot toward long-life metallurgical coal projects despite near-term operational headwinds. With consolidated cash reserves of NZD $141 million and zero debt, Bathurst appears well-positioned to fund its development pipeline while weathering volatility in domestic markets.

The company’s Q3 financial results reflect the dual pressures of declining domestic demand and moderating export coal prices. As detailed in the following summary of key financial metrics, consolidated EBITDA fell 25% year-over-year to NZD $30 million, while the company reported a consolidated operating loss of NZD $2 million compared to an $11 million profit in Q3 FY25.

Management attributed the earnings decline primarily to structural challenges in the South Island domestic segment, where key industrial customers are transitioning to alternative energy sources. This domestic weakness partially offset improved performance in the export segment, which benefited from higher sales volumes following the resolution of infrastructure constraints that had limited shipments in the prior year.

The company’s operational portfolio spans four producing mines across New Zealand, as illustrated in the following organizational structure. Bathurst maintains 100% ownership of the Takitimu domestic mine and holds 65% stakes in three joint venture operations with BT Mining: Stockton (export), Rotowaro (domestic), and Maramarua (domestic).

The geographic distribution and operational status of these assets demonstrates Bathurst’s balanced exposure to both domestic industrial markets and international steelmaking coal demand, primarily from Japan, South Korea, China, and India.

Despite the quarterly earnings contraction, management maintained its full-year EBITDA guidance, projecting consolidated EBITDA of NZD $45 million at the midpoint. The following waterfall analysis breaks down the expected drivers of year-over-year performance.

The guidance reflects a NZD $7 million improvement in export segment earnings driven by higher sales volumes, as the Tawhai tunnel closure that constrained FY25 shipments has been resolved. This positive contribution is largely offset by a NZD $7 million decline in South Island domestic operations, where customers including major industrial users are accelerating their shift away from coal-fired processes.

The company’s historical earnings profile demonstrates consistent profitability through commodity cycles, underpinned by its diversified portfolio of export and domestic operations. As shown in the following chart spanning fiscal 2018 through current guidance, Bathurst has maintained positive consolidated EBITDA throughout periods of significant metallurgical coal price volatility.

This track record reflects management’s hedging strategies, which typically secure approximately 80% of the Peak Downs Hard Coking Coal (HCC) benchmark price for export sales, providing downside protection during market weakness while retaining upside exposure during price rallies.

Metallurgical coal markets experienced notable volatility during the quarter, with HCC benchmark pricing spiking to USD $252 per tonne in January 2026 following Cyclone Koji’s disruption of Queensland supply chains. Prices subsequently moderated to USD $230 per tonne by March before stabilizing, as illustrated in the following market analysis.

Management noted that while the US-Iran conflict has had minimal direct impact on coal demand, elevated freight and fuel costs have created a higher floor price for seaborne coal while simultaneously pressuring miner margins. Forward curves suggest pricing will remain relatively stable through the second half of FY26 and into FY27, supporting the company’s earnings guidance.

The presentation emphasized Bathurst’s strategic pivot toward capital-efficient growth projects that leverage existing infrastructure while extending the company’s reserve life and production profile. Management has suspended dividends and share buybacks to direct capital toward advancing two wholly-owned metallurgical coal projects in New Zealand and British Columbia.

The centerpiece of the near-term growth strategy is the Buller Plateaux Continuation Project (BPCP), located just 17 kilometers from the existing Stockton operation. As detailed in the following project overview, Buller represents a capital-efficient pathway to adding 1.2 million tonnes per annum of production with a 13+ year mine life.

The project’s economics appear compelling, with an estimated post-tax NPV of NZD $323 million and 30% internal rate of return at metallurgical coal prices ranging from USD $228-300 per tonne. Critically, Buller will leverage Stockton’s existing coal handling and preparation plant, rail infrastructure, and port facilities, reducing the required startup capital to NZD $104.6 million.

The following production profile illustrates how Buller coal will blend with Stockton’s existing reserves to sustain annual production of approximately 1.2 million tonnes through 2041, significantly extending the export operation’s lifespan.

A key catalyst for the Buller project is New Zealand’s recently enacted Fast Track Approvals legislation, passed in December 2024. The streamlined regulatory process is expected to deliver a permitting decision in 2026, potentially enabling first production by early 2027. Management noted that metallurgical coal’s inclusion on New Zealand’s Critical Minerals List further supports the strategic importance of the project.

Complementing the New Zealand growth pipeline, Bathurst is advancing the 100%-owned Tenas Project in British Columbia’s established coal-producing region. As shown in the following project summary, Tenas targets annual production of 750,000 tonnes over a 22+ year mine life.

The project’s updated feasibility study, completed in October 2025, demonstrates attractive economics with a post-tax NPV of USD $269 million and 27.5% IRR. With FOB cash costs of just USD $80.48 per tonne, Tenas would rank among the lower-cost producers in the seaborne metallurgical coal market.

The project benefits from proximity to Canadian Pacific Railway’s common-user rail network and established deep-water terminals at Prince Rupert, providing efficient access to Asian steelmaking markets. Management expects the British Columbia Environmental Assessment process to conclude by mid-2028, with first production potentially following by late 2028.

A distinguishing feature of Bathurst’s investment profile is its unusually strong balance sheet for a small-cap mining company. As highlighted in the following financial snapshot, the company holds NZD $141 million in consolidated cash (including restricted deposits) with zero debt beyond minor equipment financing leases.

This cash position, representing approximately 49 cents per share in Australian dollar terms, provides substantial backing for the current AUD $0.59 share price. Management emphasized that the consolidated cash balance—which includes Bathurst’s 65% share of BT Mining joint venture cash—provides ample liquidity to fund both growth projects without requiring additional equity dilution or debt financing.

The capital structure supports management’s stated priorities of maintaining profitable base operations while directing free cash flow toward high-return development projects rather than immediate shareholder distributions. This approach represents a shift from prior years when the company returned capital through dividends and share buybacks.

Looking ahead, management outlined several near-term catalysts that could drive shareholder value. The Fast Track approval decision for Buller, expected in 2026, represents the most immediate catalyst, potentially de-risking a project with over NZD $300 million in estimated net present value. Concurrently, the completion of the Buller definitive feasibility study will provide updated capital cost estimates and project economics.

For the Tenas Project, the submission of environmental assessment documentation to British Columbia regulators marks continued progress toward permitting, with approval decisions anticipated by mid-2028. The company also retains optionality on the Crown Mountain project, where it holds a 22.8% economic interest with the right to increase its stake to 50% for an incremental CAD $106 million investment.

The company’s operating mines continue to generate cash flow to support these development activities. Management noted that the Takitimu mine’s life has been extended by two years, adding approximately NZD $2 million in annual cash flow. Export volumes from Stockton are expected to increase in the fourth quarter, supporting the full-year EBITDA guidance.

However, challenges remain in the domestic segment, where structural demand decline appears likely to continue as New Zealand industries transition to lower-emission energy sources. Management acknowledged this headwind while emphasizing that the company’s strategic focus has shifted decisively toward metallurgical coal for steelmaking—a market where demand fundamentals remain supported by limited new supply and the essential role of steel in global infrastructure and manufacturing.

The presentation also disclosed ongoing litigation with Talley’s Group Limited, a significant shareholder, regarding alleged breaches related to the BT Mining joint venture formation. While management expressed confidence in its position, the dispute introduces an element of uncertainty that investors should monitor.

With approximately 240 million shares outstanding and a market capitalization of AUD $142 million, Bathurst trades at an enterprise value of just AUD $25 million after accounting for its substantial net cash position. This valuation appears modest relative to the company’s reserve base and the potential value of its development projects, though investors must weigh execution risks, commodity price volatility, and the ongoing challenges in domestic operations against the company’s strategic repositioning toward long-life metallurgical coal assets.

title: Dollar steady as Middle East peace hopes recede
description: Dollar steady as Middle East peace hopes recede
fullText:
SINGAPORE, May 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. dollar held its ground on Tuesday as talks to end the war in the Middle East showed no signs of progress, pushing oil prices higher and worrying investors that interest rates may need to stay higher to tackle inflationary pressures.

Investors now fear that the ceasefire that has been in place since April 7 could be in danger and hostilities could resume in the conflict which began at the end of February, killing thousands and halting vital energy flows.

With the crucial Strait of Hormuz staying largely closed, Brent crude futures were up 0.3% at $104.55 a barrel. U.S. West Texas Intermediate was at $98.17 per barrel, up 0.13% on the day.[O/R]

U.S. President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was "on life support" after the latest back and forth on a proposal to end the war made clear the two sides were still far apart on a number of issues.

The currency market was muted to start the Asian session, with focus shifting to Trump’s visit to China later this week. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is also in Asia for meetings in Japan and South Korea.

The euro last bought $1.1775, while sterling was at $1.3602, both steady on the day. The dollar index, which measures the U.S. currency against six others, was at 97.98.

The dollar initially benefited from safe haven flows when the war first broke out but has since given up much of those gains and remains choppy on shaky prospects of a peace deal and a ceasefire that appears to be hanging by a thread.

Christopher Wong, currency strategist at OCBC, said Trump’s rejection of Iran’s response to the U.S. peace proposal has kept markets cautious and helped to put a floor under the dollar.

"Still, USD gains were contained, suggesting markets are not yet treating the latest headlines as a full risk-off shock," Wong said, noting a formal breakdown in diplomatic discussions or fresh military escalation could bring a bigger reaction.

Later in the day the spotlight will be on a U.S. inflation report, which is forecast to show consumer prices rose 0.6% last month after jumping 0.9% in March, according to a Reuters survey of economists. Estimates ranged from a 0.4% gain to a 0.9% rise.

The data will reinforce the view that the Federal Reserve is likely to keep interest rates unchanged for a while. Traders have priced out the prospect of rate cuts for the year compared to the two cuts expected before the Iran war broke out.

"The risk is that core inflation is stronger than consensus expectations because of spillover from energy prices to other prices such as airfares and food," said Sarah Hammoud, currency strategist at Commonwealth Bank of Australia.

"An upside surprise to U.S. core inflation will push up U.S. interest rates and the dollar."

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes was steady at 4.418% in Asian hours, after gaining 4.8 basis points on Monday. [US/]

The Japanese yen was last at 157.30 per U.S. dollar, steady on the day as traders watch out for comments from Bessent on the yen and Japan’s monetary policy.

Japan and the U.S. reaffirmed their close cooperation on currency moves, Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama told reporters after a meeting with her U.S. counterpart, Scott Bessent, on Tuesday.

Having supposedly spent nearly $63.7 billion in the current round of interventions, analysts say Tokyo may be counting on Bessent’s visit to Japan to deliver an additional jolt, whether through explicit endorsement or carefully chosen words that signal U.S. tolerance for Japan’s actions.

The Australian dollar was 0.14% lower at $0.724 ahead of the federal budget release, while the New Zealand dollar eased 0.07% at $0.5959. Bitcoin was last down 0.3% at $81,551 in early trading.

title: Australia business confidence shaken as costs climb, survey shows
description: Australia business confidence shaken as costs climb, survey shows
fullText:
SYDNEY, May 12 (Reuters) - Australian business confidence remained mired in gloom in April, a survey showed on Tuesday, as spiking energy costs from the Middle East war squeezed profit margins and pressured investment plans.

The survey from National Australia Bank showed its index of business confidence improved only marginally to -24 in April, having dived 29 points to -29 in March, the second-largest monthly fall in history.

Its measure of business conditions fell 3 points to +3, the second-lowest reading since 2020 and the fourth straight month of decline.

"The survey suggests that rising prices and pressure on margins are beginning to affect activity and investment measures, as forward orders, capex, cash flow and employment have all fallen noticeably in recent months and are sitting well below their respective long-run averages," said Michael Hayes, an economist at NAB.

Forward orders fell a further 4 points in April to be down 11 points since February and well below their long-run average. Capital expenditure slid 8 points in the largest such drop in the post-COVID period.

Measures of costs all rose sharply in the month, with purchase costs up 4.5% at a quarterly pace and well above selling prices at 1.8%. Retail price growth climbed to 3.2%, from 0.6%.

The Reserve Bank of Australia has raised interest rates three times in a row to reach 4.35% as it battles to control stubborn inflation. Policy makers are concerned businesses will pass on rising energy costs to consumers and stoke expectations of future inflation.

title: BOJ debated near‑term rate hike at April meeting, opening door to June move
description: BOJ debated near‑term rate hike at April meeting, opening door to June move
fullText:
TOKYO, May 12 (Reuters) - Some Bank of Japan policymakers argued in April for raising rates soon, with one flagging the chance of a June move, highlighting a growing hawkish shift on the board as an oil shock from the Iran war raised pressure for near-term tightening.

The benchmark 10-year Japanese government bond yield climbed to a 29-year high on Tuesday after a summary of opinions from last month’s meeting revealed an increasingly hawkish tone.

"It is quite possible the BOJ will raise interest rates from the next meeting onward, even if the future course of the Middle East conflict remains unclear," one board member was quoted as saying in the summary.

"While there is no need to take hasty action at this point, the BOJ should raise rates soon barring evident signs of an economic slowdown," another opinion showed.

With the BOJ’s policy rate still distant from levels deemed neutral to the economy, the BOJ must raise rates at intervals of a few months, and accelerate the pace of hikes "without hesitation" if inflationary risks rise, a third opinion showed.

The remarks heighten the chance the central bank will raise its policy rate at its next policy meeting on June 15-16.

At the April 27-28 meeting, the BOJ kept its short-term policy rate steady at 0.75% but a hawkish split on the board underscored mounting concern over inflationary pressures from the Middle East conflict.

Three of the nine board members pushed for an interest-rate hike, a proposal that was rejected but did lead to a sharp upgrade in the bank’s inflation forecasts.

Many BOJ policymakers said the Iran war was intensifying inflation pressures, raising the risk of second-round effects and bringing forward the timing for underlying inflation to reach 2%, the summary showed.

"With the outlook for prices being revised significantly upward, uncertainty regarding the situation in the Middle East remains high, and all scenarios point to further upside risks to prices," one member was quoted as saying.

"Moreover, supply-side constraints, if they materialize, will exert extremely strong upward pressure on prices," the member said.
